items.q: If E°cell for a redox reaction is positive, what does that tell you?
items.correct: The reaction is thermodynamically feasible — but it may still be very slow if kinetic barriers are high.
items.misconception: A positive E°cell guarantees the reaction will happen rapidly at room temperature.
items.random_wrong: A positive E°cell means the reaction is exothermic in every case.
items.why: Students equate 'feasible' with 'fast', missing the thermodynamics-vs-kinetics distinction (same error as with negative ΔG).

items.q: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
items.correct: Graphite has delocalised electrons between layers; diamond has all four outer electrons in localised covalent bonds.
items.misconception: Graphite conducts because it is a metal, while diamond is a non-metal.
items.random_wrong: Graphite conducts because it can melt easily at room temperature.
items.why: Students associate 'conducts electricity' with 'is a metal' and don't recognise that some non-metallic allotropes have free electrons too.

items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.
The answer examiners want is: CN⁻. “H⁺” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Members of a homologous series share:
items.correct: The same functional group and general formula
items.why: It's The same functional group and general formula. “The same molecular mass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
